attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Start Here — Implementation Guide
2	
3	**Spec:** `claude-code-mastery`
4	**Audience:** a Claude Code session (and its human) picking up this spec to build or maintain
5	the course. **You likely have no memory of how this spec was written** — this document and
6	[`decisions.md`](./decisions.md) are how that context is restored.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. What this project is (in one paragraph)
11	
12	A self-paced, solo, hands-on **course** that takes a seasoned developer who is new to
13	AI-assisted coding to elite Claude Code practitioner. It is **outcome-driven** (organized around
14	real use cases, not a feature tour), **tiered** for fast-learning senior engineers, and
15	**version-resilient** (targets the latest CLI; built to be refreshed as the tool changes — using
16	this spec). Assessment is a single capstone. See [`requirements.md`](./requirements.md) §1.
17	
18	## 2. Read order (do this first, every fresh session)
19	
20	1. **This file** — orientation + how to work within context limits.
21	2. [`decisions.md`](./decisions.md) — *why* the spec is the way it is; the rejected alternatives.
22	   Read before "improving" any requirement.
23	3. [`requirements.md`](./requirements.md) — the authoritative WHAT (R1–R15, EARS). Stable IDs.
24	4. [`design.md`](./design.md) — the HOW (architecture, schemas, artifact inventory, traceability).
25	5. [`tasks.md`](./tasks.md) — the ordered, chunked build plan.
26	
27	The persistent project memory (auto-loaded each session) also points here. Trust the **files**
28	over memory for specifics; memory is a pointer, the spec is the source of truth.
29	
30	## 3. Current state
31	
32	> **Update obligation:** this table is the **first thing a fresh session reads** — refresh it at
33	> every phase boundary (and the matching `tasks.md` status header). A stale §3 silently misleads
34	> the next session. Open loops live in the canonical ledger: `decisions.md` → "Open loops & deferrals 🔓".
35	
36	| Phase | File | Status |
37	|---|---|---|
38	| 1. Requirements | `requirements.md` | ✅ **APPROVED** (2026-05-29) — reviewed turn-by-turn, internally consistent, gap-free IDs |
39	| 2. Design | `design.md` | ✅ **APPROVED & merged to `main`** (2026-05-29; merge commit) — §0–§11 complete; design gate passed |
40	| 3. Tasks | `tasks.md` | 🟦 **IN PROGRESS** (2026-05-30) — per-phase files `tasks/P1–P6*.md` authored & traced. **P1 ✅ P2 ✅ P3 ✅ P4 ✅** (P4: both lab codebases built — `taskflow-api` 36 pytest green on `main`; `taskflow-cli` messy + 3 seeded bugs; `SEEDED.md` + offline `fixtures/mock_api.py`); **P5 in progress — U1 ✅, U2 ✅, U3 ✅, U4 ✅, U5 ✅, U6 ✅, U7 ✅, U8 ✅** (U1 onboarding/first-win: `unit.md` + lab `u01-lab1` + baseline config + refs `start/u01-lab1`/`solution/u01-lab1`, C1/C2 lab-traced. U2 explore-a-codebase: `unit.md`, C3, W8-light, **read-only** lab w/ answer key — no tag/branch/verifier. U3 operate-safely: dedicated security unit (R10.AC1), `unit.md` covers permissions/secrets/injection/blast-radius/verification + managed-settings awareness; **safety-fenced read-only prompt-injection lab** `u03-lab1` w/ fixture + plan-mode fence + objective `verify.sh` (R10.AC3/AC8), no start/solution refs like U2; C4/CV + areas 3,4,5,6,29 traced. `search-refs` still unverified; U3 now also consumes unverified `checkpoint-rewind`/`managed-settings` (L1). U4 memory-and-context: home of config/memory teaching (R11.AC4) — `CLAUDE.md`/`/context`/`settings.json` + output-styles awareness; **A/B memory lab** `u04-lab1` (read-mostly, CV via context inspector, reverts with `git restore`); dogfoods this repo's own `CLAUDE.md` (R14.AC1); C5 + areas 7,8,26,28 traced; consumes unverified `context-cmds`/`output-styles`. U5 ship-a-feature: flagship **explore→plan→code→commit** (W1) — references generalized W1 in `meta/workflows.md` (R5.AC5), teaches plan mode as a review gate (area 9) + extended-thinking awareness (area 10), two CV gates (plan, then diff); **build-a-feature lab** `u05-lab1` ships `GET /projects/{id}/stats` (write-path like U1) with `start/u05-lab1` tag + `solution/u05-lab1` branch + objective `verify.sh` (contract + ownership-404 + suite-green against the learner's working tree); C6 lab-traced; areas 9,10 traced; consumes **verified** `plan-mode`/`thinking` keys; foreign-project-404 edge satisfies woven-CV R10.AC7 for this workflow lab.) U6 tdd: red→green test-first (W2, area 11) — write the failing test, **confirm red for the right reason**, implement to green, read the impl (a test can be satisfied the wrong way); **test-first lab** `u06-lab1` adds an `overdue` filter to `GET /tasks` (overdue iff `due_date` strictly before today **and** `status != done`; done/due-today/no-due-date excluded) with `start/u06-lab1` tag + `solution/u06-lab1` branch + objective `verify.sh` (contract + no-regression suite-green vs working tree); C7 lab-traced; consumes `test-run` (kept `unverified` under L1); edge-case assertions are the woven CV. U7 debug-a-failure: scientific-method debugging (W3, area 12) — reproduce → capture → **confirm root cause** (resist the AI's plausible symptom-patch) → fix → no-regress; **debug lab** `u07-lab1` on the **legacy** `taskflow-cli` fixes baked-in bug D1 (overdue never flags — ISO-vs-`MM-DD-YYYY` string compare, copy-pasted in 3 sites) at its root, with `start/u07-lab1` tag (bug present) + `solution/u07-lab1` branch (D1 fix) + subprocess `verify.sh` (legacy has no pytest) checking overdue flagged across `list --overdue`/`stats`/display, future/no-due/done excluded, CLI intact; verified end-to-end (fails on start = automated repro); C8 lab-traced, area 12 traced, no version-specific keys (no L1 debt). U8 git-and-pr: W4 turn-work-into-a-reviewable-PR (area 13) — stage deliberately into atomic commits, why-explaining messages, PR description that matches the diff, self-review as the reviewer. **Prose-self-check lab** `u08-lab1` (NO git refs/verifier, like U2/U4): make a real `archived`-flag change in `taskflow-api` review-ready, graded by an objective reviewer's checklist; optional BYO `gh pr create` stretch (non-verifiable, R7.AC8). C9 lab-traced (front matter + `## Lab`); area 13 traced; consumes **verified** `git-pr` (no L1 debt). `make check` green; U9–U16 remaining; **P6 remaining** (capstone). On branch `spec/tasks-phase`. |
41	| — | `decisions.md` | ✅ Rationale + per-phase decisions captured; hosts the open-loops ledger |
42	
43	## 4. Q1/Q2 — ✅ RESOLVED with the user (2026-05-29)
44	
45	Both blocking inputs are now decided (full record in `decisions.md` → "Design session"):
46	
47	- **Q1 ✅** — domain = **task/project tracker**; primary `taskflow-api` (FastAPI + SQLModel + pytest),
48	  legacy `taskflow-cli` (messy, untested). Concrete layout/seeded-bug inventory → design §7 / P4.
49	- **Q2 ✅** — capability map (C1–C17 + CV) and 16-unit use-case catalog **approved** (design §1/§2/§4).
50	  Unit count is **content-driven** (the "~12" was relaxed); security pulled forward to U3.
51	
52	**Remaining open** (don't guess; see `decisions.md` → Open decisions): repo structure (§9),
53	per-feature awareness/core edge cases, capstone brief menu (R8). Next Design work: §3 (workflows),
54	§5 (version architecture), §6 (unit model/schema/templates), §7 (labs/codebases), §8–§10.
55	
56	## 5. Context-management protocol (important — this is a large build)
57	
58	The full course will exceed any single context window. Work in **bounded slices**:
59	
60	- **Don't load everything.** For a given task, load: `requirements.md` (or just the relevant Rs),
61	  the relevant `design.md` section, and the one `tasks/<section>.md` you're executing. Skip the rest.
62	- **One unit at a time.** Authoring is per-unit. Each unit is self-contained (R6.AC5), so you can
63	  build unit N loading only its task file, its front-matter schema, and the cross-cutting artifacts
64	  it references — not the other units.
65	- **Cross-cutting facts live in single files** (`meta/…`), referenced by key, never duplicated
66	  (R13.AC2, R12.AC2). Read the one you need; don't inline its contents elsewhere.
67	- **Update state as you go.** Check off tasks in `tasks.md`/`tasks/*`, bump `meta/version-record.md`,
68	  and append to `decisions.md` when you make a non-obvious call. The next session reads these, not your memory.
69	- **Verify version-specific details against the installed CLI** (`--help`, `/help`, docs) — never from
70	  model memory (R12.AC3, hard rule). Record provenance (R12.AC4).
71	
72	## 6. Definition of Done for the build (v1)
73	
74	Implementation is complete when **all** hold:
75	
76	1. Every requirement R1–R15 is satisfied and referenced by ≥1 artifact (traceability check passes, R13.AC5).
77	2. Every can-do statement traces to ≥1 lab **and** ≥1 capstone-rubric dimension (R1.AC5, R13.AC5).
78	3. Every capability area in the coverage matrix is covered at its assigned tier (R4.AC2; high-leverage ⇒ lab).
79	4. Every unit conforms to the tier-appropriate template with valid machine-readable front matter (R6, R13.AC4).
80	5. Every lab has objective self-check criteria, an inspectable reference solution, and a reset path (R7).
81	6. The capstone, its brief menu, exemplar, and self-applicable rubric exist (R8).
82	7. The two sample codebases exist and pass their own test suites (R7.AC1–AC2).
83	8. The required enforcement suite + traceability checks run green locally and in CI (R13.AC4–AC6).
84	9. The version-data file, version-record, and drift-detection check exist and pass (R12).
85	10. Dogfooding artifacts exist and are accurately referenced; the build case study + transparency note exist (R14).
86	11. The "when you're stuck" recovery resources and the learner progress checklist exist (R9.AC4–AC5).
87	
88	**Mechanical gate (added P3):** `make check-strict` green is the single automated signal that the
89	front-matter, coverage, version-reference, and traceability obligations (items 1–4 above) are *all*
90	met — it turns the build-time `PENDING` markers (not-yet-authored labs / rubric / requirement refs)
91	into hard failures. `make check` (non-strict) is the day-to-day authoring guard and stays green
92	throughout the build; **`make check-strict` is expected to fail until P6 completes** and must pass
93	for v1 done. (Items 5–7, 10 — codebases, capstone artifacts, case study — are verified by their own
94	P4/P6 checks and review, not by the static suite.)
95	
96	## 7. Maintenance (when the CLI changes later)
97	
98	Follow the R12.AC7 refresh process: run the drift-detection check → re-verify the flagged
99	version-specific details against the installed CLI → update **only** `meta/version-data.*` and any
100	truly affected locations → bump `meta/version-record.md` → re-run the enforcement suite. Because
101	version-specifics are quarantined (R12.AC2/AC8), a version bump should touch a bounded set of files,
102	not the prose.

**Spec:** `claude-code-mastery`
**Audience:** a Claude Code session (and its human) picking up this spec to build or maintain
the course. **You likely have no memory of how this spec was written** — this document and
[`decisions.md`](./decisions.md) are how that context is restored.

---

## 1. What this project is (in one paragraph)

A self-paced, solo, hands-on **course** that takes a seasoned developer who is new to
AI-assisted coding to elite Claude Code practitioner. It is **outcome-driven** (organized around
real use cases, not a feature tour), **tiered** for fast-learning senior engineers, and
**version-resilient** (targets the latest CLI; built to be refreshed as the tool changes — using
this spec). Assessment is a single capstone. See [`requirements.md`](./requirements.md) §1.

## 2. Read order (do this first, every fresh session)

1. **This file** — orientation + how to work within context limits.
2. [`decisions.md`](./decisions.md) — *why* the spec is the way it is; the rejected alternatives.
   Read before "improving" any requirement.
3. [`requirements.md`](./requirements.md) — the authoritative WHAT (R1–R15, EARS). Stable IDs.
4. [`design.md`](./design.md) — the HOW (architecture, schemas, artifact inventory, traceability).
5. [`tasks.md`](./tasks.md) — the ordered, chunked build plan.

The persistent project memory (auto-loaded each session) also points here. Trust the **files**
over memory for specifics; memory is a pointer, the spec is the source of truth.

## 3. Current state

> **Update obligation:** this table is the **first thing a fresh session reads** — refresh it at
> every phase boundary (and the matching `tasks.md` status header). A stale §3 silently misleads
> the next session. Open loops live in the canonical ledger: `decisions.md` → "Open loops & deferrals 🔓".

| Phase | File | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Requirements | `requirements.md` | ✅ **APPROVED** (2026-05-29) — reviewed turn-by-turn, internally consistent, gap-free IDs |
| 2. Design | `design.md` | ✅ **APPROVED & merged to `main`** (2026-05-29; merge commit) — §0–§11 complete; design gate passed |
| 3. Tasks | `tasks.md` | 🟦 **IN PROGRESS** (2026-05-30) — per-phase files `tasks/P1–P6*.md` authored & traced. **P1 ✅ P2 ✅ P3 ✅ P4 ✅** (P4: both lab codebases built — `taskflow-api` 36 pytest green on `main`; `taskflow-cli` messy + 3 seeded bugs; `SEEDED.md` + offline `fixtures/mock_api.py`); **P5 in progress — U1 ✅, U2 ✅, U3 ✅, U4 ✅, U5 ✅, U6 ✅, U7 ✅, U8 ✅** (U1 onboarding/first-win: `unit.md` + lab `u01-lab1` + baseline config + refs `start/u01-lab1`/`solution/u01-lab1`, C1/C2 lab-traced. U2 explore-a-codebase: `unit.md`, C3, W8-light, **read-only** lab w/ answer key — no tag/branch/verifier. U3 operate-safely: dedicated security unit (R10.AC1), `unit.md` covers permissions/secrets/injection/blast-radius/verification + managed-settings awareness; **safety-fenced read-only prompt-injection lab** `u03-lab1` w/ fixture + plan-mode fence + objective `verify.sh` (R10.AC3/AC8), no start/solution refs like U2; C4/CV + areas 3,4,5,6,29 traced. `search-refs` still unverified; U3 now also consumes unverified `checkpoint-rewind`/`managed-settings` (L1). U4 memory-and-context: home of config/memory teaching (R11.AC4) — `CLAUDE.md`/`/context`/`settings.json` + output-styles awareness; **A/B memory lab** `u04-lab1` (read-mostly, CV via context inspector, reverts with `git restore`); dogfoods this repo's own `CLAUDE.md` (R14.AC1); C5 + areas 7,8,26,28 traced; consumes unverified `context-cmds`/`output-styles`. U5 ship-a-feature: flagship **explore→plan→code→commit** (W1) — references generalized W1 in `meta/workflows.md` (R5.AC5), teaches plan mode as a review gate (area 9) + extended-thinking awareness (area 10), two CV gates (plan, then diff); **build-a-feature lab** `u05-lab1` ships `GET /projects/{id}/stats` (write-path like U1) with `start/u05-lab1` tag + `solution/u05-lab1` branch + objective `verify.sh` (contract + ownership-404 + suite-green against the learner's working tree); C6 lab-traced; areas 9,10 traced; consumes **verified** `plan-mode`/`thinking` keys; foreign-project-404 edge satisfies woven-CV R10.AC7 for this workflow lab.) U6 tdd: red→green test-first (W2, area 11) — write the failing test, **confirm red for the right reason**, implement to green, read the impl (a test can be satisfied the wrong way); **test-first lab** `u06-lab1` adds an `overdue` filter to `GET /tasks` (overdue iff `due_date` strictly before today **and** `status != done`; done/due-today/no-due-date excluded) with `start/u06-lab1` tag + `solution/u06-lab1` branch + objective `verify.sh` (contract + no-regression suite-green vs working tree); C7 lab-traced; consumes `test-run` (kept `unverified` under L1); edge-case assertions are the woven CV. U7 debug-a-failure: scientific-method debugging (W3, area 12) — reproduce → capture → **confirm root cause** (resist the AI's plausible symptom-patch) → fix → no-regress; **debug lab** `u07-lab1` on the **legacy** `taskflow-cli` fixes baked-in bug D1 (overdue never flags — ISO-vs-`MM-DD-YYYY` string compare, copy-pasted in 3 sites) at its root, with `start/u07-lab1` tag (bug present) + `solution/u07-lab1` branch (D1 fix) + subprocess `verify.sh` (legacy has no pytest) checking overdue flagged across `list --overdue`/`stats`/display, future/no-due/done excluded, CLI intact; verified end-to-end (fails on start = automated repro); C8 lab-traced, area 12 traced, no version-specific keys (no L1 debt). On branch `spec/tasks-phase`. |
| — | `decisions.md` | ✅ Rationale + per-phase decisions captured; hosts the open-loops ledger |

## 4. Q1/Q2 — ✅ RESOLVED with the user (2026-05-29)

Both blocking inputs are now decided (full record in `decisions.md` → "Design session"):

- **Q1 ✅** — domain = **task/project tracker**; primary `taskflow-api` (FastAPI + SQLModel + pytest),
  legacy `taskflow-cli` (messy, untested). Concrete layout/seeded-bug inventory → design §7 / P4.
- **Q2 ✅** — capability map (C1–C17 + CV) and 16-unit use-case catalog **approved** (design §1/§2/§4).
  Unit count is **content-driven** (the "~12" was relaxed); security pulled forward to U3.

**Remaining open** (don't guess; see `decisions.md` → Open decisions): repo structure (§9),
per-feature awareness/core edge cases, capstone brief menu (R8). Next Design work: §3 (workflows),
§5 (version architecture), §6 (unit model/schema/templates), §7 (labs/codebases), §8–§10.

## 5. Context-management protocol (important — this is a large build)

The full course will exceed any single context window. Work in **bounded slices**:

- **Don't load everything.** For a given task, load: `requirements.md` (or just the relevant Rs),
  the relevant `design.md` section, and the one `tasks/<section>.md` you're executing. Skip the rest.
- **One unit at a time.** Authoring is per-unit. Each unit is self-contained (R6.AC5), so you can
  build unit N loading only its task file, its front-matter schema, and the cross-cutting artifacts
  it references — not the other units.
- **Cross-cutting facts live in single files** (`meta/…`), referenced by key, never duplicated
  (R13.AC2, R12.AC2). Read the one you need; don't inline its contents elsewhere.
- **Update state as you go.** Check off tasks in `tasks.md`/`tasks/*`, bump `meta/version-record.md`,
  and append to `decisions.md` when you make a non-obvious call. The next session reads these, not your memory.
- **Verify version-specific details against the installed CLI** (`--help`, `/help`, docs) — never from
  model memory (R12.AC3, hard rule). Record provenance (R12.AC4).

## 6. Definition of Done for the build (v1)

Implementation is complete when **all** hold:

1. Every requirement R1–R15 is satisfied and referenced by ≥1 artifact (traceability check passes, R13.AC5).
2. Every can-do statement traces to ≥1 lab **and** ≥1 capstone-rubric dimension (R1.AC5, R13.AC5).
3. Every capability area in the coverage matrix is covered at its assigned tier (R4.AC2; high-leverage ⇒ lab).
4. Every unit conforms to the tier-appropriate template with valid machine-readable front matter (R6, R13.AC4).
5. Every lab has objective self-check criteria, an inspectable reference solution, and a reset path (R7).
6. The capstone, its brief menu, exemplar, and self-applicable rubric exist (R8).
7. The two sample codebases exist and pass their own test suites (R7.AC1–AC2).
8. The required enforcement suite + traceability checks run green locally and in CI (R13.AC4–AC6).
9. The version-data file, version-record, and drift-detection check exist and pass (R12).
10. Dogfooding artifacts exist and are accurately referenced; the build case study + transparency note exist (R14).
11. The "when you're stuck" recovery resources and the learner progress checklist exist (R9.AC4–AC5).

**Mechanical gate (added P3):** `make check-strict` green is the single automated signal that the
front-matter, coverage, version-reference, and traceability obligations (items 1–4 above) are *all*
met — it turns the build-time `PENDING` markers (not-yet-authored labs / rubric / requirement refs)
into hard failures. `make check` (non-strict) is the day-to-day authoring guard and stays green
throughout the build; **`make check-strict` is expected to fail until P6 completes** and must pass
for v1 done. (Items 5–7, 10 — codebases, capstone artifacts, case study — are verified by their own
P4/P6 checks and review, not by the static suite.)

## 7. Maintenance (when the CLI changes later)

Follow the R12.AC7 refresh process: run the drift-detection check → re-verify the flagged
version-specific details against the installed CLI → update **only** `meta/version-data.*` and any
truly affected locations → bump `meta/version-record.md` → re-run the enforcement suite. Because
version-specifics are quarantined (R12.AC2/AC8), a version bump should touch a bounded set of files,

content.content:
1	---
2	id: U8
3	title: "Turn a body of work into clean commits and a reviewable PR"
4	stage: daily-driver
5	depth_tier: core
6	use_case: "Turn my work into a clean, reviewable PR"
7	can_do: [C9]
8	workflows: [W4]
9	coverage_areas: [13]
10	prerequisites: [U5]
11	reading_time_min: 9
12	lab_time_min: 25
13	---
14	
15	# Turn a body of work into clean commits and a reviewable PR
16	
17	## Learning objectives
18	
19	By the end of this unit you can:
20	
21	- **Turn a pile of work into clean, logically atomic commits** with messages that explain *why*, and
22	  a PR whose description matches the actual diff — advances `C9`.
23	- **Stage deliberately** — decide what belongs in each commit rather than `git add -A`-ing everything,
24	  and keep unrelated changes out of a feature's history.
25	- **Self-review the PR as if you were the reviewer** before requesting one — reading the full diff and
26	  confirming the description makes no claims the code doesn't back up (`CV` applied to shareable work).
27	- **Use Claude to draft the history and the PR, then verify it** — drive `git` and `gh` through the
28	  Bash tool, but own the final shape of the commits and the description.
29	
30	## Fast path (TL;DR)
31	
32	> The Git/PR workflow is [W4](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow): turn work into history
33	> someone can *review*. **Stage deliberately** (each commit is one logical change), **write messages
34	> that explain why** (not "fix stuff"), **open a PR whose description matches the diff**, and
35	> **self-review it first** — read the whole diff as the reviewer would. Claude can draft all of this —
36	> the commit splits, the messages, the PR body via {{vd:git-pr}} — but *you* confirm the description
37	> claims only what the code does. The lab has you take a real change in `taskflow-api` and make it
38	> review-ready; the self-check is a reviewer's checklist you apply to your own diff.
39	
40	## Skip-check
41	
42	**Skip this unit if you can already:** take a body of uncommitted (or messily committed) work and turn
43	it into a reviewable PR — commits that are individually atomic and explain *why*, a description that
44	matches the diff with no aspirational claims, and a self-review pass you do *before* asking anyone else
45	— rather than dumping everything into one `git commit -am "updates"` and a PR titled "changes."
46	
47	## Concept
48	
49	You can ship a verified change (U5–U7). U8 is about the last mile: making that change **reviewable** —
50	because on any real team, work that can't be reviewed efficiently doesn't land. The unit of review
51	isn't the file, it's the **commit** and the **PR**, and both are artifacts you *author*, not exhaust
52	the tool produces.
53	
54	**The workflow — W4.** The generalized pattern lives once in
55	[`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow); read it there. The short
56	version: **stage deliberately → commit with intent → open a PR whose description matches the diff →
57	self-review before requesting review.** The rest of this section is what's specific to *applying* it
58	with Claude.
59	
60	**1 — Stage deliberately; one logical change per commit.** The fastest way to make a diff
61	un-reviewable is to bundle unrelated changes — a feature, a drive-by rename, and a formatting sweep —
62	into one commit. Decide what each commit *is* and stage only that (`git add -p` exists for exactly
63	this). When a feature needs a preparatory tidy (extract a helper, rename a symbol), that prep is its
64	**own** commit, landed first — so the reviewer reads "here's the cleanup, now here's the feature
65	riding on it," not a 400-line blob where the two are tangled.
66	
67	**2 — Write the message for the reader six months from now.** A good commit message says *why* the
68	change exists, not just *what* the diff already shows. "Add `archived` flag to projects" restates the
69	diff; "Let users hide finished projects from the default list without deleting them" explains the
70	decision. Claude will happily generate either — ask it for the *why*, and reject "update code" /
71	"fix bug" boilerplate.
72	
73	**3 — Open a PR whose description matches the diff.** PR creation is `gh` through the Bash tool
74	({{vd:git-pr}}); Claude can draft the title and body for you. The description should tell the reviewer
75	**what changed and why, how to verify it, and what you deliberately left out** — and every claim in it
76	must be true of the actual diff. The most common defect here is the *aspirational* description: a PR
77	body that describes the feature you intended, including the test you meant to write but didn't. Drafted
78	by an AI from your prompt rather than from the diff, that gap is easy to introduce — which is exactly
79	why step 4 exists.
80	
81	**4 — Self-review before you request review (CV).** Read the full diff yourself, as the reviewer
82	would — `git diff main...HEAD`, top to bottom. Does every hunk belong? Did a debug print or a stray
83	file sneak in? Does the description match what you're actually seeing? Catching your own loose ends
84	here is faster and cheaper than a review round-trip, and it's the same verification reflex from U5–U7
85	pointed at the artifact you're about to ask a human to spend attention on. ({{vd:git-pr}} also lets you
86	resume a session against an existing PR with `--from-pr`, handy when you come back to address review.)
87	
88	> **Claude drafts; you sign.** Commit messages and PR descriptions are *claims about your code* with
89	> your name on them. Let Claude write the first draft of all of it — then read the diff and make the
90	> words match the code. An unread AI-drafted PR description is how false claims get your sign-off.
91	
92	**Version currency.** This unit was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`; the `gh`
93	integration and the `--from-pr` flag are the version-specific surface ({{vd:git-pr}}) — confirm with
94	`claude --help` and see [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) if your version
95	differs. `git` and `gh` themselves are external tools Claude drives through the Bash tool.
96	
97	## Worked example
98	
99	This repository is built exactly this way, and its history is the worked example. The design phase
100	landed on `main` through a single reviewed merge once the design gate passed (not a trickle of
101	unreviewed commits to `main`); each unit since lands as a focused commit whose message names the unit
102	and explains the *why*, with the reference solution kept on its own `solution/uNN-labM` branch rather
103	than mixed into the teaching commit. Run `git log --oneline` in this repo and you'll see the pattern
104	the lab asks you to produce: one logical change per commit, messages you can read a year later, and
105	work separated into branches a reviewer can take in at a glance. (That separation — teaching commit
106	vs. solution branch — is itself a "stage deliberately" decision: the two are unrelated audiences, so
107	they don't share a commit.)
108	
109	## Lab
110	
111	> **This lab has no automated verifier.** Commit and PR *quality* is a judgment call, and a real PR
112	> needs a remote and `gh` that this course can't assume (R7.AC7). So the self-check below is an
113	> objective **reviewer's checklist** you apply to your own work — applying it *is* the skill W4
114	> teaches. An optional stretch uses real `gh` on your own remote.
115	
116	**Goal:** take a real change in `taskflow-api` and make it **review-ready** — clean commits, accurate
117	PR description, self-reviewed — without bundling unrelated work or over-claiming in the description.
118	
119	**Starting state:** the clean primary codebase on a fresh branch. Work in your own clone:
120	`cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api`, then `git switch -c u08-archived-projects` (your branch, in your
121	clone — nothing here gets pushed for you).
122	
123	**The change (your work to make reviewable):** add an **`archived`** flag to projects.
124	
125	- Add `archived: bool` (default `False`) to the `Project` model and its read/update schemas.
126	- Add an `?include_archived=` filter to the project list (default **excludes** archived projects),
127	  mirroring the existing task-list filters.
128	- If doing this cleanly needs a small preparatory tidy (e.g. extracting or renaming a filter helper),
129	  **make that a separate commit, landed first.**
130	
131	**Steps:**
132	
133	1. Drive the change with the loop you already know (explore → plan → code, U5; lean on tests, U6).
134	   Don't commit yet — let the work accumulate in the working tree.
135	2. **Stage deliberately.** Split the work into logical commits: any preparatory tidy first, then the
136	   feature. Use `git add -p` to keep each commit to one change. Ask Claude to propose the split and the
137	   messages, then edit them so each message says *why*.
138	3. **Draft the PR description** (have Claude draft it *from the diff* — `git diff main...HEAD` — not from
139	   your original prompt): what changed, why, how to verify (`pytest`), and anything deliberately out of
140	   scope.
141	4. **Self-review.** Read the full diff top to bottom as the reviewer. Fix anything that doesn't belong;
142	   make the description match the diff exactly.
143	5. *(Optional stretch — BYO, non-verifiable, R7.AC8):* on your **own** fork/remote, push the branch and
144	   `gh pr create` for real, then read the rendered PR. Never push to a remote you don't own.
145	
146	**Self-check (objective checklist — R7.AC3):** your work is review-ready when **all** hold:
147	
148	- [ ] `pytest` is green, and **every** commit builds on its own (no commit leaves the suite broken).
149	- [ ] Each commit is **one logical change** — no feature bundled with an unrelated rename/format sweep;
150	      the prep tidy (if any) is its own commit, ordered before the feature.
151	- [ ] Every commit **message explains why**, not just what — none is "update", "fix", or "wip".
152	- [ ] The PR description's claims are **all true of `git diff main...HEAD`** — no described-but-absent
153	      test, no feature you didn't actually build.
154	- [ ] The diff contains **only** the change — no debug prints, commented-out code, or stray files.
155	- [ ] `include_archived` defaults to **excluding** archived projects (the contract above), and a test
156	      pins it.
157	
158	**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch for this lab — the artifact is *your* history and PR, and
159	the checklist above is the rubric. Compare your commit series and description against it directly.
160	
161	## Common pitfalls
162	
163	- **`git commit -am "updates"`.** One giant commit with a contentless message is the default failure.
164	  The reviewer can't review it and neither can future-you. Split and explain.
165	- **Bundling unrelated changes.** A feature plus a drive-by rename plus a format sweep in one commit
166	  triples the review cost. Keep them separate; land prep first.
167	- **The aspirational PR description.** Describing what you *meant* to do — including the test you
168	  skipped. Draft the body from the diff, not the plan, and self-review catches the gap.
169	- **`git add -A` then trusting it.** Staging everything sweeps in debug prints, scratch files, and
170	  unrelated edits. Stage deliberately (`git add -p`) and read what you staged.
171	- **Letting Claude's draft message/description ship unread.** It's a claim with your name on it. Read
172	  the diff and make the words true before you commit or open the PR.
173	- **Skipping self-review.** "The reviewer will catch it" wastes a round-trip on things you'd have seen
174	  in thirty seconds reading your own diff.
175	
176	## Going deeper
177	
178	- **Next:** the Force-Multiplier stage (U9 refactor, U10 spec-driven, U11 review) all assume you can
179	  produce reviewable history; U11 (code & security review) is the other side of this coin — *being* the
180	  reviewer. U16 (automate & scale) drives PRs headlessly in CI.
181	- [`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow) — the generalized W4 pattern.
182	- The `gh` integration and `--from-pr` — {{vd:git-pr}}; version-specifics in
183	  [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md).
184	- The verify-before-you-ship reflex traces straight back to [U5](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s diff
185	  review and [U7](../07-debug-a-failure/unit.md)'s "don't claim it's fixed until it's proven."
186	- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
187	  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
188

# Turn a body of work into clean commits and a reviewable PR

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Turn a pile of work into clean, logically atomic commits** with messages that explain *why*, and
  a PR whose description matches the actual diff — advances `C9`.
- **Stage deliberately** — decide what belongs in each commit rather than `git add -A`-ing everything,
  and keep unrelated changes out of a feature's history.
- **Self-review the PR as if you were the reviewer** before requesting one — reading the full diff and
  confirming the description makes no claims the code doesn't back up (`CV` applied to shareable work).
- **Use Claude to draft the history and the PR, then verify it** — drive `git` and `gh` through the
  Bash tool, but own the final shape of the commits and the description.

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> The Git/PR workflow is [W4](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow): turn work into history
> someone can *review*. **Stage deliberately** (each commit is one logical change), **write messages
> that explain why** (not "fix stuff"), **open a PR whose description matches the diff**, and
> **self-review it first** — read the whole diff as the reviewer would. Claude can draft all of this —
> the commit splits, the messages, the PR body via {{vd:git-pr}} — but *you* confirm the description
> claims only what the code does. The lab has you take a real change in `taskflow-api` and make it
> review-ready; the self-check is a reviewer's checklist you apply to your own diff.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** take a body of uncommitted (or messily committed) work and turn
it into a reviewable PR — commits that are individually atomic and explain *why*, a description that
matches the diff with no aspirational claims, and a self-review pass you do *before* asking anyone else
— rather than dumping everything into one `git commit -am "updates"` and a PR titled "changes."

## Concept

You can ship a verified change (U5–U7). U8 is about the last mile: making that change **reviewable** —
because on any real team, work that can't be reviewed efficiently doesn't land. The unit of review
isn't the file, it's the **commit** and the **PR**, and both are artifacts you *author*, not exhaust
the tool produces.

**The workflow — W4.** The generalized pattern lives once in
[`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow); read it there. The short
version: **stage deliberately → commit with intent → open a PR whose description matches the diff →
self-review before requesting review.** The rest of this section is what's specific to *applying* it
with Claude.

**1 — Stage deliberately; one logical change per commit.** The fastest way to make a diff
un-reviewable is to bundle unrelated changes — a feature, a drive-by rename, and a formatting sweep —
into one commit. Decide what each commit *is* and stage only that (`git add -p` exists for exactly
this). When a feature needs a preparatory tidy (extract a helper, rename a symbol), that prep is its
**own** commit, landed first — so the reviewer reads "here's the cleanup, now here's the feature
riding on it," not a 400-line blob where the two are tangled.

**2 — Write the message for the reader six months from now.** A good commit message says *why* the
change exists, not just *what* the diff already shows. "Add `archived` flag to projects" restates the
diff; "Let users hide finished projects from the default list without deleting them" explains the
decision. Claude will happily generate either — ask it for the *why*, and reject "update code" /
"fix bug" boilerplate.

**3 — Open a PR whose description matches the diff.** PR creation is `gh` through the Bash tool
({{vd:git-pr}}); Claude can draft the title and body for you. The description should tell the reviewer
**what changed and why, how to verify it, and what you deliberately left out** — and every claim in it
must be true of the actual diff. The most common defect here is the *aspirational* description: a PR
body that describes the feature you intended, including the test you meant to write but didn't. Drafted
by an AI from your prompt rather than from the diff, that gap is easy to introduce — which is exactly
why step 4 exists.

**4 — Self-review before you request review (CV).** Read the full diff yourself, as the reviewer
would — `git diff main...HEAD`, top to bottom. Does every hunk belong? Did a debug print or a stray
file sneak in? Does the description match what you're actually seeing? Catching your own loose ends
here is faster and cheaper than a review round-trip, and it's the same verification reflex from U5–U7
pointed at the artifact you're about to ask a human to spend attention on. ({{vd:git-pr}} also lets you
resume a session against an existing PR with `--from-pr`, handy when you come back to address review.)

> **Claude drafts; you sign.** Commit messages and PR descriptions are *claims about your code* with
> your name on them. Let Claude write the first draft of all of it — then read the diff and make the
> words match the code. An unread AI-drafted PR description is how false claims get your sign-off.

**Version currency.** This unit was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`; the `gh`
integration and the `--from-pr` flag are the version-specific surface ({{vd:git-pr}}) — confirm with
`claude --help` and see [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) if your version
differs. `git` and `gh` themselves are external tools Claude drives through the Bash tool.

## Worked example

This repository is built exactly this way, and its history is the worked example. The design phase
landed on `main` through a single reviewed merge once the design gate passed (not a trickle of
unreviewed commits to `main`); each unit since lands as a focused commit whose message names the unit
and explains the *why*, with the reference solution kept on its own `solution/uNN-labM` branch rather
than mixed into the teaching commit. Run `git log --oneline` in this repo and you'll see the pattern
the lab asks you to produce: one logical change per commit, messages you can read a year later, and
work separated into branches a reviewer can take in at a glance. (That separation — teaching commit
vs. solution branch — is itself a "stage deliberately" decision: the two are unrelated audiences, so
they don't share a commit.)

## Lab

> **This lab has no automated verifier.** Commit and PR *quality* is a judgment call, and a real PR
> needs a remote and `gh` that this course can't assume (R7.AC7). So the self-check below is an
> objective **reviewer's checklist** you apply to your own work — applying it *is* the skill W4
> teaches. An optional stretch uses real `gh` on your own remote.

**Goal:** take a real change in `taskflow-api` and make it **review-ready** — clean commits, accurate
PR description, self-reviewed — without bundling unrelated work or over-claiming in the description.

**Starting state:** the clean primary codebase on a fresh branch. Work in your own clone:
`cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api`, then `git switch -c u08-archived-projects` (your branch, in your
clone — nothing here gets pushed for you).

**The change (your work to make reviewable):** add an **`archived`** flag to projects.

- Add `archived: bool` (default `False`) to the `Project` model and its read/update schemas.
- Add an `?include_archived=` filter to the project list (default **excludes** archived projects),
  mirroring the existing task-list filters.
- If doing this cleanly needs a small preparatory tidy (e.g. extracting or renaming a filter helper),
  **make that a separate commit, landed first.**

**Steps:**

1. Drive the change with the loop you already know (explore → plan → code, U5; lean on tests, U6).
   Don't commit yet — let the work accumulate in the working tree.
2. **Stage deliberately.** Split the work into logical commits: any preparatory tidy first, then the
   feature. Use `git add -p` to keep each commit to one change. Ask Claude to propose the split and the
   messages, then edit them so each message says *why*.
3. **Draft the PR description** (have Claude draft it *from the diff* — `git diff main...HEAD` — not from
   your original prompt): what changed, why, how to verify (`pytest`), and anything deliberately out of
   scope.
4. **Self-review.** Read the full diff top to bottom as the reviewer. Fix anything that doesn't belong;
   make the description match the diff exactly.
5. *(Optional stretch — BYO, non-verifiable, R7.AC8):* on your **own** fork/remote, push the branch and
   `gh pr create` for real, then read the rendered PR. Never push to a remote you don't own.

**Self-check (objective checklist — R7.AC3):** your work is review-ready when **all** hold:

- [ ] `pytest` is green, and **every** commit builds on its own (no commit leaves the suite broken).
- [ ] Each commit is **one logical change** — no feature bundled with an unrelated rename/format sweep;
      the prep tidy (if any) is its own commit, ordered before the feature.
- [ ] Every commit **message explains why**, not just what — none is "update", "fix", or "wip".
- [ ] The PR description's claims are **all true of `git diff main...HEAD`** — no described-but-absent
      test, no feature you didn't actually build.
- [ ] The diff contains **only** the change — no debug prints, commented-out code, or stray files.
- [ ] `include_archived` defaults to **excluding** archived projects (the contract above), and a test
      pins it.

**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch for this lab — the artifact is *your* history and PR, and
the checklist above is the rubric. Compare your commit series and description against it directly.

## Common pitfalls

- **`git commit -am "updates"`.** One giant commit with a contentless message is the default failure.
  The reviewer can't review it and neither can future-you. Split and explain.
- **Bundling unrelated changes.** A feature plus a drive-by rename plus a format sweep in one commit
  triples the review cost. Keep them separate; land prep first.
- **The aspirational PR description.** Describing what you *meant* to do — including the test you
  skipped. Draft the body from the diff, not the plan, and self-review catches the gap.
- **`git add -A` then trusting it.** Staging everything sweeps in debug prints, scratch files, and
  unrelated edits. Stage deliberately (`git add -p`) and read what you staged.
- **Letting Claude's draft message/description ship unread.** It's a claim with your name on it. Read
  the diff and make the words true before you commit or open the PR.
- **Skipping self-review.** "The reviewer will catch it" wastes a round-trip on things you'd have seen
  in thirty seconds reading your own diff.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** the Force-Multiplier stage (U9 refactor, U10 spec-driven, U11 review) all assume you can
  produce reviewable history; U11 (code & security review) is the other side of this coin — *being* the
  reviewer. U16 (automate & scale) drives PRs headlessly in CI.
- [`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w4--git--pr-workflow) — the generalized W4 pattern.
- The `gh` integration and `--from-pr` — {{vd:git-pr}}; version-specifics in
  [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md).
- The verify-before-you-ship reflex traces straight back to [U5](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s diff
  review and [U7](../07-debug-a-failure/unit.md)'s "don't claim it's fixed until it's proven."
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).

1	# Workflow methods (W1–W9)
2	
3	The nine named, reusable methods the course teaches **inside** their use cases, then
4	generalizes (R3.AC4). No workflow is a free-floating lesson — each maps to ≥1 exercising unit
5	(R3.AC5). Units **reference** this file for the generalized pattern rather than re-explaining
6	it (R5.AC5). Stage tags match `meta/capability-map.yaml` and `design.md` §3.
7	
8	Source of truth: `design.md` §3. **[R3, R5.AC5]**
9	
10	## Summary table
11	
12	| # | Workflow | Stage | When to choose it | Expected verification (CV) | Unit(s) |
13	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
14	| W1 | explore → plan → code → commit | daily-driver | Default for any non-trivial change | Review the **plan** before coding; read the diff + run tests before commit | U5 |
15	| W2 | test-driven development | daily-driver | Behavior is specifiable as a test; regression-prone or bug-fix work | Confirm the test fails first **for the right reason** (red), then green; review the impl | U6 |
16	| W3 | debugging an unfamiliar failure | daily-driver | Something is broken and the cause is unknown | Reproduce first; capture the bug in a failing test; confirm root cause, then that the fix doesn't regress | U7 |
17	| W4 | Git / PR workflow | daily-driver | Turning work into shareable history/review | Review the staged diff; ensure message matches change; self-review the PR | U8 |
18	| W5 | multi-file refactoring | force-multiplier | Cross-cutting change touching many files; rename/restructure | Behavior-preserving: full suite green **before and after**; diff-review for scope creep | U9 |
19	| W6 | code & security review | force-multiplier | Before merging a significant or security-sensitive change | The workflow *is* verification — but cross-check findings, don't trust the review blindly | U11 |
20	| W7 | spec-driven development | force-multiplier | Large/ambiguous feature where upfront alignment pays off | Each phase gate reviewed/approved before the next; trace requirements→design→tasks | U10 |
21	| W8 | onboarding to an unfamiliar/large codebase | first-wins *(light)* → force-multiplier *(deep)* | Entering code you don't know | Validate Claude's architecture summary against the actual code (spot-check claims) | U2 (light), U9 (deep) |
22	| W9 | running parallel agents (git worktrees) | autonomy-scale | Multiple independent tasks that can run concurrently | Isolate each in a worktree; review each agent's diff independently before integrating | U16 |
23	
24	**Reconciliation notes (R3.AC2/AC6):**
25	- W7 / U10 uses **this very `specs/claude-code-mastery/`** spec as its worked example (R3.AC2).
26	- W8 is intentionally two-tiered — light exploration (U2, primary repo) vs deep
27	  onboarding-for-refactor (U9, legacy repo); see `meta/use-case-catalog.yaml`.
28	- Every workflow has ≥1 exercising unit; no catalog row is better expressed as a workflow.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Generalized patterns
33	
34	Each pattern below is the durable, version-independent method. Units link here and add only the
35	unit-specific application (the concrete prompts and the lab).
36	
37	### W1 — explore → plan → code → commit
38	**The pattern.** Resist jumping straight to code. (1) **Explore**: have Claude read the relevant
39	code and restate the task and constraints. (2) **Plan**: get an explicit plan you can review
40	*before* any edit (use plan mode so nothing is written yet). (3) **Code**: implement against the
41	approved plan in small steps. (4) **Commit**: read the diff, run the tests, then commit.
42	**Choose it when:** any change big enough that a wrong turn costs more than the planning does —
43	the default for non-trivial work. **Verify (CV):** the gate is the *plan*; reject or redirect a
44	bad plan before code exists. Then read the diff and run tests before committing.
45	
46	### W2 — test-driven development
47	**The pattern.** Write (or have Claude write) a test that pins the desired behavior, run it, and
48	**confirm it fails for the right reason** (not a typo/import error). Then implement just enough to
49	go green. Refactor with the test as a safety net. **Choose it when:** the behavior is
50	specifiable as a test, or the work is regression-prone or a bug fix. **Verify (CV):** red-then-
51	green is the verification — but read the implementation, since a test can be satisfied the wrong
52	way (e.g., hard-coded return).
53	
54	### W3 — debugging an unfamiliar failure
55	**The pattern.** Be a scientist, not a guesser. (1) **Reproduce** the failure deterministically.
56	(2) Capture it in a **failing test** so "fixed" is objective. (3) Form hypotheses, instrument,
57	and **confirm the root cause** before changing anything. (4) Fix, then confirm the test passes
58	**and** nothing else regressed. **Choose it when:** something is broken and the cause is unknown.
59	**Verify (CV):** a passing test you wrote from the repro, plus a green full suite — never a fix
60	justified only by "it looks right."
61	
62	### W4 — Git / PR workflow
63	**The pattern.** Turn a pile of work into reviewable history: stage deliberately, write commits
64	that explain *why*, and open a PR whose description matches the actual diff. Self-review the PR as
65	if you were the reviewer before requesting one. **Choose it when:** sharing work for review or
66	preserving history. **Verify (CV):** read the staged diff; confirm the message/PR description
67	matches what changed (no aspirational claims); self-review surfaces issues before others do.
68	
69	### W5 — multi-file refactoring
70	**The pattern.** A behavior-preserving change across many files. Establish a green baseline,
71	make the structural change in reviewable increments, and keep the suite green throughout.
72	Watch for scope creep — refactor and behavior change should not mix in one diff. **Choose it
73	when:** a cross-cutting rename/restructure touches many files. **Verify (CV):** full suite green
74	**before and after**; diff-review specifically for behavior changes that snuck into a "pure"
75	refactor.
76	
77	### W6 — code & security review
78	**The pattern.** Before merging a significant or security-sensitive change, run a structured
79	review (correctness *and* security) over the diff. Treat findings as leads, triage them, and
80	fix or justify each. **Choose it when:** a change is significant or touches security-sensitive
81	surface (auth, input handling, secrets, untrusted content). **Verify (CV):** the review *is* a
82	verification step — but cross-check its findings against the code; an automated review both
83	misses real issues and raises false positives, so don't accept or dismiss blindly.
84	
85	### W7 — spec-driven development
86	**The pattern.** For a large/ambiguous feature, align *before* building: write **requirements**,
87	then **design**, then **tasks**, with an explicit approval gate between phases. Trace each later
88	artifact back to the requirement it serves. **Choose it when:** the cost of building the wrong
89	thing is high and upfront alignment pays off. **Verify (CV):** each phase gate is reviewed and
90	approved before the next begins; requirements→design→tasks traceability is checkable.
91	**Worked example:** this course's own `specs/claude-code-mastery/` (R3.AC2).
92	
93	### W8 — onboarding to an unfamiliar/large codebase
94	**The pattern.** Before changing code you don't know, build a map: have Claude summarize the
95	architecture, entry points, and data flow, then **validate that summary against the actual code**
96	before trusting it. *Light* onboarding (enough to locate a change site) vs *deep* onboarding
97	(enough to safely refactor) differ in depth, not method. **Choose it when:** entering code you
98	don't know. **Verify (CV):** spot-check Claude's architecture claims against the source — an
99	onboarding summary is a hypothesis until checked.
100	
101	### W9 — running parallel agents (git worktrees)
102	**The pattern.** When independent tasks can run concurrently, give each its own **git worktree**
103	so agents don't collide on the working tree, run them in parallel, then review and integrate each
104	diff separately. **Choose it when:** multiple independent tasks can proceed at once and isolation
105	is worth the setup. **Verify (CV):** review each agent's diff independently before integrating;
106	isolation makes a bad result containable rather than entangled.

# Workflow methods (W1–W9)

The nine named, reusable methods the course teaches **inside** their use cases, then
generalizes (R3.AC4). No workflow is a free-floating lesson — each maps to ≥1 exercising unit
(R3.AC5). Units **reference** this file for the generalized pattern rather than re-explaining
it (R5.AC5). Stage tags match `meta/capability-map.yaml` and `design.md` §3.

Source of truth: `design.md` §3. **[R3, R5.AC5]**

## Summary table

| # | Workflow | Stage | When to choose it | Expected verification (CV) | Unit(s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | explore → plan → code → commit | daily-driver | Default for any non-trivial change | Review the **plan** before coding; read the diff + run tests before commit | U5 |
| W2 | test-driven development | daily-driver | Behavior is specifiable as a test; regression-prone or bug-fix work | Confirm the test fails first **for the right reason** (red), then green; review the impl | U6 |
| W3 | debugging an unfamiliar failure | daily-driver | Something is broken and the cause is unknown | Reproduce first; capture the bug in a failing test; confirm root cause, then that the fix doesn't regress | U7 |
| W4 | Git / PR workflow | daily-driver | Turning work into shareable history/review | Review the staged diff; ensure message matches change; self-review the PR | U8 |
| W5 | multi-file refactoring | force-multiplier | Cross-cutting change touching many files; rename/restructure | Behavior-preserving: full suite green **before and after**; diff-review for scope creep | U9 |
| W6 | code & security review | force-multiplier | Before merging a significant or security-sensitive change | The workflow *is* verification — but cross-check findings, don't trust the review blindly | U11 |
| W7 | spec-driven development | force-multiplier | Large/ambiguous feature where upfront alignment pays off | Each phase gate reviewed/approved before the next; trace requirements→design→tasks | U10 |
| W8 | onboarding to an unfamiliar/large codebase | first-wins *(light)* → force-multiplier *(deep)* | Entering code you don't know | Validate Claude's architecture summary against the actual code (spot-check claims) | U2 (light), U9 (deep) |
| W9 | running parallel agents (git worktrees) | autonomy-scale | Multiple independent tasks that can run concurrently | Isolate each in a worktree; review each agent's diff independently before integrating | U16 |

**Reconciliation notes (R3.AC2/AC6):**
- W7 / U10 uses **this very `specs/claude-code-mastery/`** spec as its worked example (R3.AC2).
- W8 is intentionally two-tiered — light exploration (U2, primary repo) vs deep
  onboarding-for-refactor (U9, legacy repo); see `meta/use-case-catalog.yaml`.
- Every workflow has ≥1 exercising unit; no catalog row is better expressed as a workflow.

---

## Generalized patterns

Each pattern below is the durable, version-independent method. Units link here and add only the
unit-specific application (the concrete prompts and the lab).

### W1 — explore → plan → code → commit
**The pattern.** Resist jumping straight to code. (1) **Explore**: have Claude read the relevant
code and restate the task and constraints. (2) **Plan**: get an explicit plan you can review
*before* any edit (use plan mode so nothing is written yet). (3) **Code**: implement against the
approved plan in small steps. (4) **Commit**: read the diff, run the tests, then commit.
**Choose it when:** any change big enough that a wrong turn costs more than the planning does —
the default for non-trivial work. **Verify (CV):** the gate is the *plan*; reject or redirect a
bad plan before code exists. Then read the diff and run tests before committing.

### W2 — test-driven development
**The pattern.** Write (or have Claude write) a test that pins the desired behavior, run it, and
**confirm it fails for the right reason** (not a typo/import error). Then implement just enough to
go green. Refactor with the test as a safety net. **Choose it when:** the behavior is
specifiable as a test, or the work is regression-prone or a bug fix. **Verify (CV):** red-then-
green is the verification — but read the implementation, since a test can be satisfied the wrong
way (e.g., hard-coded return).

### W3 — debugging an unfamiliar failure
**The pattern.** Be a scientist, not a guesser. (1) **Reproduce** the failure deterministically.
(2) Capture it in a **failing test** so "fixed" is objective. (3) Form hypotheses, instrument,
and **confirm the root cause** before changing anything. (4) Fix, then confirm the test passes
**and** nothing else regressed. **Choose it when:** something is broken and the cause is unknown.
**Verify (CV):** a passing test you wrote from the repro, plus a green full suite — never a fix
justified only by "it looks right."

### W4 — Git / PR workflow
**The pattern.** Turn a pile of work into reviewable history: stage deliberately, write commits
that explain *why*, and open a PR whose description matches the actual diff. Self-review the PR as
if you were the reviewer before requesting one. **Choose it when:** sharing work for review or
preserving history. **Verify (CV):** read the staged diff; confirm the message/PR description
matches what changed (no aspirational claims); self-review surfaces issues before others do.

### W5 — multi-file refactoring
**The pattern.** A behavior-preserving change across many files. Establish a green baseline,
make the structural change in reviewable increments, and keep the suite green throughout.
Watch for scope creep — refactor and behavior change should not mix in one diff. **Choose it
when:** a cross-cutting rename/restructure touches many files. **Verify (CV):** full suite green
**before and after**; diff-review specifically for behavior changes that snuck into a "pure"
refactor.

### W6 — code & security review
**The pattern.** Before merging a significant or security-sensitive change, run a structured
review (correctness *and* security) over the diff. Treat findings as leads, triage them, and
fix or justify each. **Choose it when:** a change is significant or touches security-sensitive
surface (auth, input handling, secrets, untrusted content). **Verify (CV):** the review *is* a
verification step — but cross-check its findings against the code; an automated review both
misses real issues and raises false positives, so don't accept or dismiss blindly.

### W7 — spec-driven development
**The pattern.** For a large/ambiguous feature, align *before* building: write **requirements**,
then **design**, then **tasks**, with an explicit approval gate between phases. Trace each later
artifact back to the requirement it serves. **Choose it when:** the cost of building the wrong
thing is high and upfront alignment pays off. **Verify (CV):** each phase gate is reviewed and
approved before the next begins; requirements→design→tasks traceability is checkable.
**Worked example:** this course's own `specs/claude-code-mastery/` (R3.AC2).

### W8 — onboarding to an unfamiliar/large codebase
**The pattern.** Before changing code you don't know, build a map: have Claude summarize the
architecture, entry points, and data flow, then **validate that summary against the actual code**
before trusting it. *Light* onboarding (enough to locate a change site) vs *deep* onboarding
(enough to safely refactor) differ in depth, not method. **Choose it when:** entering code you
don't know. **Verify (CV):** spot-check Claude's architecture claims against the source — an
onboarding summary is a hypothesis until checked.

### W9 — running parallel agents (git worktrees)
**The pattern.** When independent tasks can run concurrently, give each its own **git worktree**
so agents don't collide on the working tree, run them in parallel, then review and integrate each
diff separately. **Choose it when:** multiple independent tasks can proceed at once and isolation
is worth the setup. **Verify (CV):** review each agent's diff independently before integrating;
isolation makes a bad result containable rather than entangled.

1	---
2	id: U7
3	title: "Debug an unfamiliar failure by confirming the root cause"
4	stage: daily-driver
5	depth_tier: core
6	use_case: "A test/endpoint is broken — find and fix it"
7	can_do: [C8]
8	workflows: [W3]
9	coverage_areas: [12]
10	prerequisites: [U5]
11	reading_time_min: 9
12	lab_time_min: 30
13	---
14	
15	# Debug an unfamiliar failure by confirming the root cause
16	
17	## Learning objectives
18	
19	By the end of this unit you can:
20	
21	- **Debug an unfamiliar failure as a scientist, not a guesser** — reproduce it deterministically,
22	  confirm the *root cause* before changing anything, fix it, and prove the fix — advances `C8`.
23	- **Capture the failure as a repro you can re-run** so "fixed" is objective, not a vibe.
24	- **Confirm the root cause rather than pattern-matching the symptom** — and resist the AI's pull
25	  toward a plausible-looking change that treats a symptom and leaves the cause (and its copies) intact.
26	- **Verify the fix *and* that nothing regressed** — the bug is gone, the repro passes, and the rest
27	  of the program still works (`CV`).
28	
29	## Fast path (TL;DR)
30	
31	> Debugging with Claude is [W3](../../../meta/workflows.md#w3--debugging-an-unfamiliar-failure):
32	> **reproduce → capture → confirm root cause → fix → no-regress.** Get a deterministic repro first;
33	> have Claude (and you) **find the real cause** before touching code — the #1 failure mode is letting
34	> a confident AI patch a symptom. Then fix at the cause, re-run the repro to confirm it's gone, and
35	> check nothing else broke. The lab hands you a real bug in the messy `taskflow-cli`: **overdue tasks
36	> are never flagged.** You'll chase it to its root — and discover the cause is copy-pasted in three
37	> places, so a one-site "fix" leaves the bug alive. `tools/verify-lab u07-lab1` proves you got it.
38	
39	## Skip-check
40	
41	**Skip this unit if you can already:** take a bug you've never seen, **reproduce it deterministically**,
42	drive Claude to **confirm the actual root cause** (with evidence, not a guess) before any edit, fix it
43	at the cause, and verify both that the repro now passes **and** that nothing else regressed — rather
44	than accepting the first plausible-looking patch Claude proposes for the symptom.
45	
46	## Concept
47	
48	In U5 and U6 you *added* behavior; debugging is the inverse skill — something already works wrong and
49	you don't yet know why. The discipline that separates fixing from flailing is the **scientific
50	method**, and it matters *more* with an AI in the loop, not less. Claude is extremely good at producing
51	a confident, plausible change in response to a symptom — and a plausible change that treats the symptom
52	without addressing the cause is the most expensive kind of wrong, because it *looks* fixed. Your job is
53	to keep the loop honest: reproduce, find the cause, *then* change code.
54	
55	**The workflow — W3.** The generalized pattern lives once in
56	[`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w3--debugging-an-unfamiliar-failure); read it there.
57	The short version: **reproduce deterministically → capture it as a failing repro → confirm the root
58	cause → fix → confirm the repro passes and nothing regressed.** The rest of this section is what's
59	specific to *applying* it with Claude.
60	
61	**1 — Reproduce deterministically, first.** A bug you can't reproduce on demand, you can't prove fixed.
62	Pin down the exact command, input, and expected-vs-actual *before* you theorize. For the lab: a task
63	with a due date in the past should be flagged overdue — `list --overdue` should show it, `stats` should
64	count it — and it isn't. That's your repro: concrete, repeatable, observable.
65	
66	**2 — Capture the failure so "fixed" is objective.** Turn the repro into something you can re-run on
67	command — a failing test, or at minimum a scripted command sequence with a known expected output. This
68	is the W2 reflex (U6) pointed at a bug: the captured failure is what flips from red to green to *prove*
69	the fix, and it guards the bug from silently coming back. (The legacy CLI has no test suite at all —
70	which means the repro you capture here is its *first* characterization test, a thread U9 picks up.)
71	
72	**3 — Confirm the root cause before changing anything.** This is the step the whole unit turns on.
73	Have Claude investigate and **explain the cause with evidence** — the specific line and *why* it
74	produces the wrong result — and make it convince *you* before it edits. In the lab the symptom
75	("overdue never flags") has a non-obvious cause: the code compares the stored date (ISO `YYYY-MM-DD`)
76	against today rendered as `MM-DD-YYYY`, as plain strings — a comparison that's meaningless across two
77	formats. A guesser sees "overdue is wrong" and tweaks something nearby; a debugger finds *that line*.
78	And here's the trap the lab is built around: **that broken comparison is copy-pasted in three places.**
79	Fixing the one Claude happens to open first makes the symptom partly disappear and leaves the bug alive
80	elsewhere. Confirming the root cause means finding *all* of it.
81	
82	**4 — Fix at the cause, then verify the fix and the absence of regressions (CV).** Once the cause is
83	confirmed, fix it *there* — compare the dates as dates, at every site (the clean move is to fix the one
84	real check and route the others through it). Then: re-run your repro (the overdue task is now flagged),
85	**and** confirm nothing else broke — adding, listing, and counting tasks still behave. A debugging
86	change that fixes the bug and quietly breaks a neighbor is not done. Green repro **plus** a working
87	program — verified, not assumed — is the bar.
88	
89	> **Reproduce before you trust a "fix."** The single most common way AI-assisted debugging goes wrong:
90	> the symptom changes, everyone declares victory, and the cause is still there (or moved). Your repro
91	> from step 2 is the antidote — it's not fixed until that goes green and stays green.
92	
93	**Version currency.** Nothing in this unit depends on a version-specific Claude Code surface — it's
94	the debugging *method* plus tool-use you already have (running the program / its tests via the Bash
95	tool). See [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) if your CLI differs.
96	
97	## Worked example
98	
99	This course's own build debugged exactly this way. When `check-version-refs` first went in, a unit
100	referenced a `vd:` key that didn't resolve — but the *symptom* showed up far away, as a confusing
101	"unresolved key: key" failure. The fix wasn't to silence the message; it was to **reproduce** it on the
102	offending file, **confirm the root cause** (a unit had written a doubled-brace `vd:` token *literally*,
103	as a prose illustration, which the checker can't tell from a real reference), and fix it at the cause —
104	reword the illustration —
105	rather than weakening the check. The tell was the same one this unit teaches: the first plausible
106	explanation ("the checker is too strict") treated the symptom; the real cause was the content, and
107	confirming it before editing is what kept the check honest. (That exact failure happened authoring U6.)
108	
109	## Lab
110	
111	**Goal:** find and fix a real bug in the legacy `taskflow-cli` by confirming its root cause — reproduce
112	it, capture it, fix it at the cause (everywhere it lives), and verify the fix without regressing the
113	rest of the CLI.
114	
115	**The bug (your repro):** in `codebases/legacy/taskflow-cli`, **overdue tasks are never flagged.** A
116	task whose due date is in the past and isn't done should be reported as overdue — but:
117	
118	- `python taskflow.py list --overdue` returns nothing, and
119	- `python taskflow.py stats` reports `overdue: 0`,
120	
121	even with an obviously past-due task in the database. Expected: that task is flagged overdue, while a
122	task due in the future, a task with no due date, and a *done* task are not.
123	
124	**Starting state:** `start/u07-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u07-lab1` to restore it) — the legacy CLI
125	with the bug present (it's a real, baked-in landmine; the CLI has no test suite).
126	
127	**Steps:**
128	
129	1. `tools/reset-lab u07-lab1`, then start a session in the legacy codebase:
130	   `cd codebases/legacy/taskflow-cli && claude`. Use a throwaway DB so you don't touch real data:
131	   `export TASKFLOW_DB=$(mktemp)`.
132	2. **Reproduce.** Add a past-due task (`python taskflow.py add "Pay invoice" --due 2020-01-01`), then
133	   `list --overdue` and `stats`. Confirm the symptom: nothing flagged, `overdue: 0`. That's your repro.
134	3. **Capture.** Write it down as a re-runnable check (a failing test, or a scripted sequence with the
135	   expected output) so "fixed" will be objective and the bug can't sneak back.
136	4. **Confirm the root cause — before any edit.** Have Claude locate *why* overdue never fires and
137	   explain it with the specific line and reason. Push for the real cause (how the dates are compared),
138	   not a nearby guess. **Then ask: where else does this same logic live?** Confirm you've found every
139	   copy before fixing — a partial fix is the trap.
140	5. **Fix and verify (CV).** Fix the cause at every site, re-run your repro (the past-due task is now
141	   flagged in `list --overdue`, `stats` shows `overdue: 1`, and the listing/`show` display mark it),
142	   and confirm a future-due, no-due, and done task are *not* flagged — and that add/list/stats still
143	   work. Commit with a message that names the root cause.
144	
145	**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u07-lab1`. It passes only if a past-due, not-done task
146	is flagged overdue across the surfaces that report it (`list --overdue`, `stats`, and the `list`/`show`
147	display), future/no-due/done tasks are correctly excluded, and the CLI's basic add/list/stats behavior
148	still works. (It fails on the unfixed starting state — that's the repro, automated.)
149	
150	**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u07-lab1` — diagnose unaided first, then diff against it. (It
151	fixes the date comparison to compare dates as dates and routes the duplicated checks through the single
152	`is_overdue` helper, so the cause is fixed at every site rather than one. Yours may differ in shape and
153	still pass — the verifier checks behavior, not structure.)
154	
155	**Verify (CV):** the repro from step 3 is the gate. The bug is fixed when that repro goes green **and**
156	stays green while the rest of the CLI keeps working — not when the first symptom disappears. The
157	verifier deliberately checks the *display* surfaces too, so a fix applied to only one of the three
158	copies fails: confirming the root cause means fixing all of it.
159	
160	## Common pitfalls
161	
162	- **Fixing before reproducing.** If you can't reproduce it on command, you can't prove you fixed it.
163	  Get the deterministic repro first — always.
164	- **Accepting the first plausible patch.** Claude will happily propose a confident change for the
165	  symptom. A change that makes the symptom go away is not the same as a confirmed root cause — make it
166	  show you *why* the bug happens before it edits.
167	- **Fixing one copy of a duplicated bug.** The lab's bug lives in three places. Fix the one you found
168	  and the symptom only half-disappears (e.g. the count works but the display still doesn't). "Confirm
169	  the root cause" includes "find every instance of it."
170	- **Treating green-here as done.** A fix that resolves the bug but breaks a neighbor is a regression you
171	  introduced. Re-run the repro *and* exercise the surrounding behavior before you call it fixed.
172	- **Skipping the captured repro.** Eyeballing "looks fixed now" lets the bug return silently. The
173	  re-runnable repro is what makes the fix durable.
174	
175	## Going deeper
176	
177	- **Next:** U8 (Git/PR) turns the verified fix into reviewable history; U9 (onboard + refactor) returns
178	  to this same messy `taskflow-cli` and untangles the duplication that *let* this bug hide in three
179	  places — debugging and refactoring are two views of the same code smell.
180	- [`meta/workflows.md`](../../../meta/workflows.md#w3--debugging-an-unfamiliar-failure) — the generalized
181	  W3 pattern, and how it reuses the failing-test reflex from [W2](../06-tdd/unit.md).
182	- The capture-as-a-test habit is [U6](../06-tdd/unit.md)'s red→green pointed at a bug; the diff-reading
183	  and verification reflexes are [U5](../05-ship-a-feature/unit.md)'s.
184	- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
185	  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).

title: "Debug an unfamiliar failure by confirming the root cause"
stage: daily-driver
depth_tier: core

workflows: [W3]
